steps.title: Find the main difficulty
steps.content: The passage says simply copying one walkability feature from San Francisco may not increase walking elsewhere. Reyes explains why: walking decisions depend on complex contexts involving demographics, public transit, conditions, and individual needs. The San Francisco example supports that broader difficulty.

steps.title: Reject narrower readings
steps.content: Choice B is about comparing traffic counts between cities, which the passage does not do. Choice C focuses too narrowly on storefront density. Choice D invents a way to improve individual decision-making. The answer is A.
